original_hypothesis.title: BlastOnboarding proxy can be bricked or have funds stolen via owner setting bootstrapper to malicious implementation (delegatecall abuse)
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: BlastOnboarding is an OZ Proxy whose _implementation() returns `bootstrapper`. Owner can change `bootstrapper` at any time. A malicious or compromised owner can set bootstrapper to an implementation that, via delegatecall, overwrites storage (supportedTokens, balances, feeTo) or transfers all held ERC20 balances out, draining onboarding deposits. This is an upgrade-key risk inherent to the design.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is not an accidental ‘owner can rug’ observation; it is a concrete mechanism: delegatecall gives implementation code full write access to proxy storage and token balances. Given BlastOnboarding holds user deposits, compromise of the upgrade key is catastrophic. Validate whether any on-chain mitigations exist (e.g., immutable bootstrapper post-close, time-lock, or restricted upgrade windows).
deployment_assumptions: Owner key is compromised OR owner behaves maliciously (outside bright-line on-chain attacker model)
Users have deposited tokens into BlastOnboarding
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: BlastOnboarding.sol exposes setBootstrapper(address) onlyOwner with no timelock/constraints; deployment scripts set supported tokens but do not constrain future upgrades.

proof_of_signal.pattern: custom Proxy delegates to bootstrapper; onlyOwner can change bootstrapper arbitrarily; implementation has full delegatecall authority over stored user balances accounting

preconditions: Proxy currently holds user-deposited ERC20 balances
Malicious implementation includes token transfer logic using proxy context

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: upgradeable proxy with unrestricted implementation pointer changes
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: delegatecall allows arbitrary storage writes and asset transfers
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No timelock, no state gating (e.g., only when Idle), no immutable implementation after close

analysis_questions: Is setBootstrapper intended to be used only once? If so, can it be locked after close/ready?
Are there off-chain operational controls (multisig) and on-chain restrictions?
Does the implementation code (BlastOnboardingBoot) assume specific storage layout that could be exploited to corrupt balances?
validation_requirements: Review all bootstrapper implementations for any callable functions that transfer arbitrary tokens
Simulate upgrade to malicious bootstrapper and demonstrate delegatecall drain in tests
Assess whether open/close states should gate upgrades

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Compromised owner sets bootstrapper to attacker implementation; attacker calls a function through proxy that delegatecalls and transfers all supported token balances to attacker.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total loss of all onboarding deposits held by the proxy

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests shown for upgrade restrictions or post-close immutability

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Upgrade event LogBootstrapperChanged followed by anomalous token transfers

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
